title: Use the slide-script-prep skill. Do not edit files. Do not use web. Author worldview matters. Task: produce a short local-only 3-slide outline about why medical AI adoption should start from auditability and shared context rather than model size. At the very end, print exactly these lines: consulted...
summary_text: Local-only note: this is a worldview-shaped outline, not a source-verified deck. Framing: domain-first over model-leaderboard framing; shared context before more AI; accountability at the clinical decision boundary. Deck take-home: In medical AI, the first adoption unit is not the biggest model. It is the smallest workflow clinicians can inspect, challenge, and carry forward together. 1. Slide 1: `Medicine does not adopt black boxes, even when they are powerful` Message: model size is not the first barrier; unreconstructable reasoning is. - A larger model can still produce an answer nobody can trace back to patient context, prior decisions, or evidence. - In clinical settings, the first question is “why this recommendation here, now, for this patient?” - If that path is invisible, trust, governance, and rollout stop before capability matters. 2. Slide 2: `Start with auditability and shared context` Message: the minimum viable medical AI stack is traceability plus a shared context layer. - Capture what the system saw, what it retrieved, what tools it used, and where a human overrode it. - Preserve shared context across shifts, teams, and encounters so the model is not reasoning from a blank slate each time. - Keep the human at the judgment boundary; AI should support a decision, not hide the basis of one. 3. Slide 3: `Earn the right to bigger models later` Message: stronger models help only after the operating model is accountable. - Stage 1: narrow, low-risk tasks with visible evidence and rollback. - Stage 2: shared context and audit trails across the workflow. - Stage 3: upgrade model capability only when it improves outcomes without weakening explanation or responsibility. Closing line: first build the system clinicians can verify together; then decide how much model power that system actually needs. consulted_worldview_source: /Users/masayuki/.claude/docs/philosophy.md used_obsidian_for_worldview: no worldview_contract_applied: true
